data.title: Historical Data: Recessions Trump Interest Rate Relief For Car And Home Sales
data.description:
Economists are worried about recession, with an average estimate of 45% probability that one will begin in the next 12 months, according to the Wall Street Journal’s recent survey. The Federal Reserve would certainly cut interest rates if that happened, as emphasized by Chair Jerome Powell’s recent comments that data will drive the Fed’s upcoming decisions.

Interest-sensitive business might be wondering if a recession would actually help them by lowering interest rates. Stimulating spending in the interest-sensitive parts of the economy is a key element of monetary policy in a recession. The historical data are disappointing, though. Spending drops in recessions, even in interest-sensitive sectors. Looking at two key sectors in detail, it’s clear that business contingency planning should focus on the downside risk of recession rather than the upside from falling interest rates.

This conclusion is driven by studying two sectors of the economy: car sales, new home sales and private non-residential construction.

Many of the country’s recessions were triggered by rising interest rates as the Fed tried to fight inflation. Those downturns occurred in 1970, 1973-75, 1980 and 1981-82. In all of these cases, car sales and home sales had dropped well before the recessions. In fact, the interest-sensitive sectors pulled the economy down in these slumps. When the Fed cut interest rates to fight those recessions, sales in both sectors revived partially, but the recessions themselves hurt activity.

Two of the more recent recessions offer better lessons. Car sales began to fall in late 1989 just after interest rates hit their peak for that cycle. Sales slumped more even as interest rates fell, then slowly regained ground as the downturn fell farther into the past. The recession of 1990 played a larger role than interest rates movements.

The economy went into recession again in 2001, frequently attributed to the end of Y2K spending and the dot-com bust. Auto sales were roughly flat even though the Fed cut its benchmark interest rate from 6.5% to 1.75%. That enabled automakers to offer zero percent financing (making up the difference between market interest and zero). There was no real decline in sales in this recession, possibly because of that sharp interest rate cut. But certainly, car dealers had no boost to sales from the lower interest rates.

New home sales in these two recessions did improve as interest rates fell, but the recession still had an impact. The data show that home sales would have been better if the recession had not occurred.

The “Great Recession” of 2008-09 was very severe. Interest were cut to near zero percent (for short maturities). But new automobile sales took four years to regain their pre-recession level even with interest rates of approximately nil.

Home sales in this era do not tell a recession-and-interest rate story. Rather, the boom in new construction in the years preceding the recession swamped the market with excess housing. It took years before new home sales recovered. In fact, they have not yet regained the peak level of 2005 despite 20 years having elapsed.

The most recent recession occurred with the pandemic. The economy peaked in February 2020, then hit its trough just two months later. Auto sales dropped sharply in the lockdown days, then recovered with some make-up sales. The Fed cut interest rates sharply in the pandemic, but sales remained low for two years. Supply chain disruptions, including computer chip shortages, may explain some limitation of sales.

New home sales did surge with low-interest rates, rising well above the pre-pandemic trend line. There’s no doubt that low financing costs helped sales. Many apartment renters who had wanted to buy a house calculated their borrowing cost at the low-interest rates and went shopping well before they otherwise would have been able to at the old interest rates.

The weight of evidence, despite the post-pandemic home sales rally, is that recessions trump low-interest rates. Business plans should certainly take into account the mitigating effects of lower interest rates during recessions, but there’s no justification for anticipating higher sales, not even in the most interest-sensitive parts of the economy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Neurocrine Biosciences: Still A Buy After 30% Uplift Since My Last Coverage
data.description:
Investment Overview - Brief Summary Of My Last Neurocrine "Buy" Call

In my last note on Neurocrine Biosciences, Inc. (NASDAQ:NBIX) for Seeking Alpha back in mid-April, I gave the San Diego-based Biotech's stock a "Buy" recommendation, sensing a contrarian trade opportunity, with shares priced at $99 - stock is up 30% since, trading at $129 at the time of writing, up just 2% on a five-year basis.

I covered some of Neurocrine's history - it IPO'd way back in 1996 - in that note, but to summarise, the company earns the vast majority of its revenues today from a single drug, INGREZZA (valbenazine) - as I wrote in April:

Ingrezza (valbenazine), a "highly selective VMAT2 inhibitor," has been approved to treat tardive dyskinesia (uncontrollable, abnormal and repetitive movements of the trunk, extremities and/or face) since 2017, and Huntington's Chorea (a hereditary progressive neurodegenerative disorder) since 2023.

INGREZZA revenues amounted to $545.2m out of $563.7m of revenues in Q1 2025, for example, with sales up 7% year-on-year. The company is profitable too - earning net income of $341.3m, 249.7m, and 154.5m in the past three years working backwards.

Nevertheless, when Neurocrine shared its full-year 2024 results in early February - total revenues of $2.3bn, and earnings per share of $3.4 on a GAAP basis - which represents a price to sales ratio of 38x - and shared full-year guidance for 2025 for $2.5 - $2.6bn of revenues, the market sold Neurocrine stock, which retreated in value from >$150 per share, to ~$115 per share.

Wall Street seemed unimpressed with guidance implying a ~10% year-on-year revenue uplift, as opposed to the 25% figure achieved in 2024, but I noted that:

Last December, Neurocrine secured approval for Crenessity (crinecerfont), a "corticotropin-releasing factor type 1 receptor antagonist," to treat congenital adrenal hyperplasia ("CAH") in adults and children aged 4+. CAH is a rare genetic condition that affects the adrenal glands.

The drug will apparently cost ~$265k per annum and analysts see it as a potential ~$1.5bn per annum peak seller.

On the Q4 earnings call management warned that while it expected Crenessity to achieve "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues in time, early revenue generation would be impacted by three main factors:

delayed timing of reimbursement for a new rare disease product, frequency of patient flow into offices, and trialing that will naturally occur as clinicians obtain real-world experience with a novel medicine like CRENESSITY

In summary, there seemed no reason to panic about underwhelming early sales of Crenessity, and Neurocrine in fact has a third asset - osavampator - in Phase 3 studies in patients showing inadequate response to treatment in major depressive disorder ("MDD"), plus two more Phase 2 stage assets indicated for schizophrenia and MDD, the M4 agonist NBI-568, and NBI-770, a "selective, and orally active, negative allosteric modulator (NAM) of the NR2B subunit-containing N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA NR2B) receptor" (Source: Neurocrine 2024 annual report/10-K submission).

I concluded that:

ultimately, the value of Products + Pipeline strikes me as closer to $15bn than $10bn. Hence, I am making Neurocrine a slight contrarian "Buy."

Updates Since Last Note - Stock Soars On Positive Q1 - Multiple New Pivotal Studies Begin

Neurocrine announced its Q1 2025 earnings in early May, prompting a jump in the already recovering share price from ~$110 per share, to >$120. Revenue highlights were as follows:

First-Quarter 2025 Net Product Sales Highlights

  • INGREZZA first-quarter 2025 net product sales were $545 million, representing 8% growth compared to first quarter 2024.
  • Year-over-year growth driven by underlying patient demand including record new patient starts and improved gross-to-net dynamics.
  • Expanded formulary access for INGREZZA, significantly improving coverage to now include two-thirds of tardive dyskinesia and Huntington's disease Medicare beneficiaries.
  • CRENESSITY first-quarter net product sales were $14.5 million and included 413 total patient enrollment start forms reflecting strong initial patient demand with approximately 70% reimbursement coverage for dispensed scripts.

Management upheld its full year guidance, and reported GAAP net income of $8m, or $0.08 per share, and non-GAAP net income of $72 million and $0.70, respectively, down from $125 million and $1.20 in the prior year period, but nevertheless, Wall Street appeared encouraged, perhaps due to the chaotic nature of the past few months within the healthcare and biotech industries, with Secretary Robert F. Kennedy appointed to lead the Department for Health and Human Services, and Dr Marty Makary appointed as the new Head of the FDA, while the Trump administration threatened to impose tariffs specifically on pharma companies importing product from overseas.

On the Q1 earnings call CEO Kyle Gano - who replaced long-term CEO Kevin, Gorman, Ph.D., last year (Gano was formerly Chief Business Development and Strategy Officer), announced the appointment of a new Chief Medical Officer, Dr. Sanjay Keswani, who replaces Dr. Eiry Roberts, who retains an ongoing role as a strategic advisor to the company.

Regarding Crenessity, $15m of revenues in its first full quarter is a solid performance, in my view, although management continued to sound a cautious note. Neurocrine's Chief Commercial Officer ("CCO") Eric Benevich told analysts:

Overall, initial metrics are trending in the right direction across all key performance indicators. But I do want to remind everyone that one quarter is too soon to define a trend for either adoption or reimbursement. We're going to learn a lot over the coming quarters specific to persistency, compliance rates and overall rate of adoption.

If CRENESSITY ultimately delivers significant benefit in the real world as it did in clinical trials, we fully believe it can become the new standard of care together with cortisol replacement for CAH patients.

News regarding osavampator also appeared positive, with Dr Roberts telling analysts:

I'm pleased to announce the initiation of all three randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled studies evaluating its efficacy and safety as an adjunctive treatment for major depressive disorder. These studies will measure the change in total MADRS from baseline to day 56 as their primary endpoint with top line data expected throughout 2027.

NBI-568 has also progressed into a Phase 3 study in schizophrenia, with two more studies expected to initiate "in the coming months", although data is not expected until 2027/28. The primary endpoint for all three studies is a comparison of the 20mg dose versus placebo in terms of reduction from baseline in the Positive and Negative Syndrome Scale ("PANSS").

Finally, there are three important data catalysts to note upcoming in 2025. Phase 3 data for valbenazine in Adjunctive Treatment of Schizophrenia, and in Dyskinetic Cerebral Palsy, plus Phase 2 NBI-770 data.

As we can see from the slide above, summarising much of what I have discussed above, Neurocrine appears to be a company teeming with activity and possibility, while at the same time delivering strong revenue and income performance, and boasting ~$1.8bn cash. It is therefore not surprising to me that shares have bounced back rapidly from their April low. However, they still trade at a 15% discount to their value of >$150, in February this year.

Does Neurocrine - market cap $12.8bn at the time of writing - merit an even higher valuation?

Analysis - Neurocrine Value Assessment Contingent Upon Competition, Pipeline Prospects

One obstacle to Neurocrine's growth - in revenues, profitability, and valuation - is its competition. INGREZZA, for example, competes with Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Limited's (TEVA) Austedo in both TD and Huntington's. Austedo earned ~$2bn in 2024, putting it on a par with INGREZZA, and in general, the two therapies appear closely matched.

Both therapies have a similar mechanism of action ("MoA"), being VMAT2 inhibitors, although INGREZZA may be taken once daily with or without food, while Austedo XR is available for once daily dosing, but only with food.

According to Drugs.com, INGREZZA's efficacy slightly edges Austedo's in both Huntington's and TD, while both come with a boxed safety warning for depression and suicidality. INGREZZA appears to be the more expensive therapy, which may be related to price controls imposed on Austedo via the Inflation Reduction Act ("IRA"), which could affect INGREZZA down the line.

The good news is that INGREZZA's patents appear to be protected until 2038, while Neurocrine says that today, "nearly 9 out of 10 TD patients are not yet treated with a VMAT2 inhibitor, out of a patient population of ~800k, so it seems both Austedo and INGREZZA could thrive for many more years to come.

Regarding Crenessity, Neurocrine says CAH has been treated with steroids as standard of care for nearly 70 years, and there are no other recently approved therapies, although management does state in its 2024 annual report/10-K submission:

In the U.S. alone, there are more than two dozen companies manufacturing steroid-based products. In addition, there are several programs in clinical development by other companies targeting CAH

Crenessity's patent protection is also secure well into the next decade, so we may reasonably conclude that Neurocrine could be a >$3.5bn revenue per annum company by 2030 based on these two assets alone.

On top of this, Neurocrine is promising to deliver a "new medicine every two years". However, it's near term targets of MDD and Schizophrenia are markets where competition is significant and approvals can be elusive. For example, in August last year Neurocrine reported NBI-568 had met the primary goal in a mid-stage study:

demonstrating a statistically significant 7.5-point improvement (p=0.011, 0.61 Effect Size) in the PANSS Total Score, compared to placebo at week 6 with an 18.2-Point PANSS Total Score improvement from baseline.

The market dumped Neurocrine stock on the news, however, which sank from >$150, to <$125 overnight, as analysts concluded Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) drug Cobenfy had generated superior data in its own studies.

BMY paid $14bn to acquire Karuna Therapeutics, developer of Cobenfy, and the drug was initially believed to be a potential >$5bn per annum asset, but a recent study failure as an adjunctive treatment to atypical antipsychotics has seen analysts slash that estimate to ~$2.6bn.

My point is that NBI-568 and NBI-770, while exciting assets with massive potential earning capacity, are by no means slam dunks for approval, and we may note the increase in R&D expenses between Q1 2024 and Q1 2025, from $159m, to $263m.

Neurocrine is investing heavily in its late stage programs, but approvals are by no means guaranteed, and even if they are secured, Cobenfy, for example, in Schixophrenia, and AbbVie Inc.'s (ABBV) >$3bn per annum selling Vraylar, in MDD, pose strong competition.

Final Analysis - After A 30% Run-up Since My April Buy Call, Am I Still Bullish On Neurocrine?

In my last note I suggested that I felt Neurocrine felt more like a $15bn valuation company than a $10bn valuation company, and now it has reached a valuation of $12.8bn, so is it worth investing today in the hope of a further ~17% uplift in the share price?

It's a tricky question to answer. Companies earning similar volumes of revenues in 2024 include BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (BMRN), $2.84bn revenues in 2024, market cap valuation $11bn, BioNTech SE (BNTX), $2.98bn, market cap $26.4bn, Royalty Pharma plc (RPRX), $2.26bn revenues, market cap $20.5bn, Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL), $2.2bn revenues, market cap $12.6bn, Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), $2.25bn, market cap $43bn, and United Therapeutics, $2.9bn revenues, market cap $13.3bn.

Since I believe Neurocrine ought to achieve $3.5bn in revenues from its two lead assets by 2030, given market underpenetration, lack of competition, and potentially, label expansions for valbenazine, I do believe Neurocrine's valuation can push on past $15bn in the medium-to-long term, and since many of the above companies have higher valuations thanks to their promising pipelines - Alnylam and BioNTech, for example, I'd rate Neurocrine's pipeline as somewhere between the promise of these two companies, and therefore capable of adding several billion to the valuation if study results are positive.

Given the company can remain profitable while funding so many late-stage studies, we may also conclude that these programs are something of a "free hit" for the company. Succeed in schizophrenia or MDD, and we could be looking at Neurocrine becoming a $5bn revenue per annum company by 2030.

I believe the above table is instructive, showing that NBI-568 has achieved an effect size in Schizophrenia almost as impressive as Cobenfy (KarXT) and superior to current marketed products in its Phase 2 study.

Bear in mind that BMY invested $14bn to gain access to Cobenfy alone, and then consider its recent study failure - then consider what kind of value could we attach to Neurocrine's candidate, that the market does not seem to be factoring into the company's current market cap valuation. Again, I'd estimate several billion dollars.

As such, and also bearing in mind, as I pointed out in my last note, Neurocrine may make for a very tempting acquisition target for a number of Big Pharma concerns - AbbVie, Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK), and perhaps even BMY itself spring to mind - I remain confident Neurocrine is somewhat undervalued, and would not necessarily be flattered by a $20bn valuation.

There are crucial catalysts upcoming across the next 12 months, so I do expect some volatility in Neurocrine's share price, however the next time I come to cover the company, in late 2025, say, I would not be surprised if the share price has increased by a further 20%, based on my research discussed in this note.

data.title: Alibaba Remains Heavily Discounted
data.description:
One company that I believe the market continues to underestimate is Chinese e-commerce and cloud computing giant Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA). Back in early April of this year, I wrote an article wherein I detailed that the business was in a prime position to capitalize on what is almost certain to be significant growth in AI in China over the next several years. Investments that the company was making into the space, as well as its history of growth in a market that has been historically very concentrated and dominated by US firms, made possible upside for shareholders. On top of this, as I detailed, shares of the business are cheaper than US competitors. But since then, the stock has languished, actually dropping 10.3% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 14.4%.

This weakness has come in spite of the fact that fundamental figures have, for the most part, continued to improve for the business year over year. The stock has gotten even cheaper and the firm's balance sheet is robust. Certain challenges remain, especially now that we have this trade war situation going on. But when you look at the long-term picture, it's difficult to be anything other than bullish about this company. Because of this, and in spite of the fact that the market has so far disagreed with my bullish assessment of it, I maintain a ‘buy’ rating.

A necessary disclosure

Historically, management has reported financial results in both Renminbi and U.S. dollars. In their quarterly and annual earnings releases, they do provide the equivalent of U.S. dollars for their major financial figures. But they don't do that for the previous windows of time covered in their latest financial releases. For the purpose of comparability, I have translated the currency for prior periods on my own, using a constant currency basis to weed out any foreign currency fluctuations baked into the firm's figures.

The picture keeps getting better

From a purely fundamental perspective, things are going quite well for Alibaba at this time. Take, for instance, results from the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company came in at $32.58 billion. That represents an increase of 6.6% compared to the $30.58 billion the business reported one year earlier. This growth in revenue for the company was driven by strength across all but one of its operating segments. For starters, we have the Taobao and Tmall Group, which involves the company’s consumer and B2B e-commerce operations. From the final quarter of 2024 to the final quarter of 2025, revenue for this unit grew 8.8% year over year.

Management claimed that customer management revenue jumped around 12%, thanks to improvements in take rate on a year-over-year basis. It appears as though continued investments in achieving a larger user base, as well as pushing through other initiatives that involve promoting price-competitive products, improving customer service, and offering attractive membership program benefits, have all played a role here as well. According to the data provided, the company reported having over 50 million 88VIP members. This is a select group of the highest-spending consumers on its platform. That compares to the roughly 35 million the company had just one year earlier. It does appear as though some of this growth, though we don't know how much, can be attributed to continued AI technology investments that the company is making as well. So even though my previous article on the company focused more on its ability to benefit on the cloud computing side, it is obvious that its e-commerce operations also stand to benefit.

This was not the only segment to perform well. As an example, the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group, which provides international commerce retail and wholesale services for its customers, particularly its AliExpress offering, reported a rise in sales from $3.78 billion to $4.63 billion. Management chalked this expansion up to strength for the firm's cross-border businesses. This comes as the company continues to benefit from additional enhancements and making investments aimed at boosting operating efficiency. The Choice side of the business is doing particularly well according to management.

Next in line, we have the Local Services Group, which is engaged in the business of food and beverage delivery, not to mention other products, for those customers who need them. Revenue for this unit grew nicely from $2.02 billion to $2.22 billion. This can be chalked up to greater revenue from marketing services, as well as additional order growth for both its Amap and Ele.me offerings.

On top of this, the Digital Media and Entertainment Group reported a rise in sales from $681 million to $765 million. This growth has been the result of strength in the movie and entertainment space, as well as the result of greater advertising revenue coming from the firm’s Youku subsidiary. For those not familiar with it, Youku operates as a long-form video platform based out of China that produces and distributes content to its audience. Honestly, the fact that the firm is seeing some growth here is encouraging. Keep in mind that it was as recent as early 2024 that management announced that they were writing down assets associated with that business and the amount of $1.2 billion.

There are two other parts of the company that I believe deserve additional attention. One of these is doing quite well, while the other is doing quite poorly. The one that's doing well is my favorite part of the business, which would be the Cloud Intelligence Group. This is the part of the company that focuses on cloud computing. Year over year, revenue for this unit shot up a solid 17.7% from $3.53 billion to $4.15 billion. Faster public cloud revenue growth, including that which involves AI-related products, ended up being responsible for this push higher.

In fact, AI-related product revenue grew at a triple-digit rate year-over-year according to management. This is the 7th consecutive quarter where it has experienced that kind of growth. To continue this kind of momentum, management is innovating rapidly. In April of this year, for instance, the firm launched its Qwen3 series, which is a new generation of hybrid reasoning models aimed at providing a quick but thorough reasoning process for its users. I would also like to point out that revenue growth is not the only thing the company is seeing here. In fact, the overall segment EBITA skyrocketed from $197 million to $333 million.

This has been a trend that I have seen in the space recently. In fact, I recently wrote articles about competitors Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Google parent Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), here and here respectively, that showed that they continue to benefit from margin expansion as their businesses grow. That this makes sense when you consider that this is a high demand industry that's growing rapidly and that there are only a few players who offer significant scale in this market. Although Alibaba is considerably smaller than Amazon, Alphabet, or Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), it is still a big player in the industry and stands to benefit from significant growth.

Not everything has been great, however. The weak spot for the company has been its Cainiao Smart Logistics Network Limited segment, which has been part of the firm that is responsible for customer fulfillment. Management had previously made it clear that it was their objective to be able to eventually facilitate delivery of any product ordered on its platforms to customers in China within 24 hours or within 72 hours for customers in international markets. Unfortunately, revenue for this unit actually contracted year over year from $3.38 billion to $2.97 billion. However, this might not be all that bad. You see, according to the company, this was not the result of weakness but instead stemmed from the continued integration of the company's logistics offerings into its e-commerce segments. This would, by necessity, take away from this segment. So honestly, I am not terribly surprised here.

With revenue rising, profits and cash flows for the company have gone up as well. Net income jumped from $451 million to $1.71 billion. On an adjusted basis, net income for the company expanded from $3.37 billion to $4.11 billion. Operating cash flow grew from $3.22 billion to $3.79 billion. Lastly, EBITDA for the company grew from $4.25 billion to $5.76 billion. In the chart above, you can see results for the entirety of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. And in the previous chart shown throughout this article, you can see the segment-by-segment data for this window of time as well. In short, we are continuing to see growth across almost all the business. And that should definitely be encouraging for investors moving forward.

To be honest with you, there is some uncertainty here when it comes to tariffs. However, the overall impact on the business might not be that large. Under its Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group, for instance, the company mentioned that international revenue amounted to $4.63 billion in the most recent quarter alone. This was a figure that we already covered. And for the year in its entirety, it accounted for $18.23 billion. Using the quarterly figures, this works out to about 14.2% of sales. However, it is important to be mindful of the fact that the company does generate revenue from several countries. AliExpress, for instance, provides B2C services to customers in over 100 markets, including the US, Russia, Brazil, Spain, and elsewhere. Other parts of the company, like Lazada, which engages in international e-commerce operations, do business in Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Singapore. Because management does not disclose figures, we can't really even guess the impact that tariffs from the US might have on the firm. But it is safe to say that it's not a large portion of the overall revenue of the business.

Based on the historical results that Alibaba has now generated over the last two fiscal years, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. But that's not all. I also compared the firm to both Amazon and Alphabet given the similarities that exist between them. And using each of these valuation approaches, it is clear that Alibaba is quite a bit cheaper. I do think it's important to note that because Alibaba is a Chinese firm and because there are legitimate issues when it comes to corruption and the risk of fraud, the firm deserves to trade at a discount to these other firms. But honestly, I find the discount it is trading at to be very significant. This is especially true when the business has a net cash position of $33.23 billion, and that's not even factoring in $49.17 billion of long-term investments. For a business with a market capitalization of $258.96 billion, this is a large amount of liquidity.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I believe that the market continues to get Alibaba wrong. Even though uncertain times could hit the business, I think that, in the long run, it should continue to benefit from multiple growth opportunities. This includes continued e-commerce growth, as well as growth in AI. Add on top of this how cheap shares are, and I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate makes sense.

data.title: Prudential: Stability, Value, And Cash Flow In One Package
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In an environment of more stable rates and margin pressure in the insurance sector, Prudential Financial (NYSE:PRU) is positioned as a defensive but value option. The company combines mature and resilient businesses with an expanding institutional asset management segment, supporting a strong and predictable return.

Q1 2025 confirmed the strength of PRU. Although net profit showed a decrease in special accounting effects, the core business performed very well. Adjusted operating income after tax grew 7% YoY ($1.19B), thanks to improvement in its key segments, especially in the US and group insurance.

In Individual Life, the company managed to reduce its operating losses. For its part, PGIM, the asset management division, maintained positive flows and increased assets under management despite lower revenue from performance commissions. In addition, PRU improved its liquidity position and maintained an attractive return for its shareholders, with a dividend yielding around 5.6%.

From a valuation point of view, PRU is traded with a P/E FWD of less than 8x, with a P/B just below 1x, and is supported by a model that balances stable earnings with upside via asset management. The stock does not promise exponential growth, but an attractive combination of operating profitability, capital efficiency and a consistent distribution policy.

In line with this, I consider it a good medium- to long-term buy option, especially for investors who prioritize cash flow, visibility and diversified exposure to the financial sector with low relative volatility.

Overview

In 2025, Prudential Financial is consolidated as one of the world's leading companies in the intersection between insurance, retirement solutions and institutional asset management.

Based in Newark and with more than 145 years of history, it combines its legacy with a clear commitment to modernize its business model. In an environment that requires capital efficiency, technology and customer focus, PRU is moving steadily forward on three main axes: growing its global asset management platform, evolving its offer in the US, and selectively expanding into markets such as Japan and Brazil.

PGIM, its investment arm, is a key driver of this strategy. With multiple specialized boutiques, it offers institutional access to real estate, fixed income, private equity, and infrastructure. In a market that migrates towards alternative assets -and where large mandates increasingly seek active management and depth-PGIM stands out for attracting sustained flows and for its direct exposure to structural issues such as energy transition or demography. More than a revenue generator, it is a strategic advantage.

In the US, PRU maintains leadership in group insurance, individual life and hybrid retirement products. Solutions like FlexGuard, which combine market exposure with some protection, reflect what many customers are looking for today: smooth performance. To this is added a strong investment in technology, which positions it as one of the most advanced insurers in process automation and digital experience. It is also adapting its offer to the new capital rules of the NAIC, redesigning its portfolio to sustain profitability with less regulatory requirements.

The international operation remains a strategic pillar. Japan, the world's second-largest insurance market, has an aging population with a high propensity to save, and PRU is well-positioned to capture this demand with long-term retirement products. At the same time, Brazil offers room for growth in a still underserved system, where digitalization and the formalization of savings point to a market with much to build.

Globally, the sector is growing at a steady pace. According to Swiss Re, premiums rise moderately, driven by the recovery in Asia and Latin America and increased awareness of financial planning. In the US alone, more than 11,000 people retire every day, which explains why retirement solutions are now a focal point for insurers like PRU.

Looking to the future, Prudential faces the same challenge as many of its peers: continue to grow in a world with stable rates, tighter margins, and competition from native digital models. But unlike others, PRU moves with discipline, without promising shortcuts. Its scale, reputation, and consistency continue to position it as one of the companies best prepared to build sustained value in a business where trust is more important than any other.

Q1 2025

In Q1 2025, PRU showed a solid operating performance, even against a lower net profit derived from extraordinary accounting factors. The company reported net attributable income of $707MM or $1.96 per share, down 38% YoY from $1.19B or $3.12 per share in the same period last year. This decline is mainly explained by losses on revaluation of profits with market risk and investment adjustments.

However, from an operational perspective, the results were positive: after-tax adjusted operating income reached $1.19B or $3.29 per share, representing a growth of 7% YoY, driven by better results in the company's core businesses.

The strength of PRU is also reflected in its balance sheet. Adjusted book value per share remained solid at $96.37, just below the previous year's $97.03. The GAAP book value rose to $83.59 from $75.00, implying an 11% improvement YoY. Assets under management (AUM) grew modestly to $1.52T (+2% YoY), benefiting from the recovery of financial markets and positive net flows.

In line with its shareholder return policy, Prudential returned $736MM in the quarter, including $486MM in dividends (equivalent to a dividend yield of 5.6% on adjusted book value) and $250MM in share buybacks. The holding's liquidity position also improved to $4.9B from $4.2B a year ago.

As for the operating segments, PGIM, the global asset management division, reported adjusted operating income of $156MM, a decline of 8% YoY, due to lower revenues from co-investments and performance fees.

However, PGIM's AUM grew 3% YoY to $1.39T, thanks to positive net flows of $4.3B, especially from institutional clients in fixed income, private equity, and equities. This demonstrates the structural resilience and institutional relevance of PGIM, despite a weaker quarter in alternative revenues.

U.S. business showed an operating growth of 16% YoY, reaching $931MM. In that segment, Retirement Strategies remained stable at $876MM, with Institutional Retirement falling to $412MM (-7% YoY) and Individual Retirement rising to $464MM (+5% YoY), driven by the sale of indexed products. Individual Retirement sales totaled $3.5B (+5% YoY).

At Group Insurance, operating income doubled to $89MM (+98% YoY), thanks to a significant improvement in life and disability outcomes. For its part, Individual Life reduced its operating loss to $34MM from $121MM since Q1 2024, with sales growing 26% YoY to $210MM.

The international segment posted operating revenues of $848MM, implying a 5% year-on-year decline, affected by lower investment margins, lower profits from joint ventures, and an adverse currency impact. Despite this, sales in constant currency grew 15% YoY to $586MM, with good performance in both Japan and Brazil, confirming the strategic value of the international business, although subject to external swings.

The corporate segment and others showed a loss of $415MM, which improved slightly compared to $435MM a year ago, thanks to a cut in expenses partially offset by a higher financial burden. The total of negative off-trade accounting adjustments reached $600MM before tax, including $351MM for change in market profits, $246MM in investment losses, and $73MM in divested or run-off businesses.

Valuation

PRU is quoted at a clear discount, although this is due to a weak growth profile and structurally low profitability. The P/E is 8.17x TTMy 7.88x FWD, against a sector average of 11.8x and 10.9x, implying a discount of 30% and 28% respectively. This compression on the multiple occurs even when EPS growth is expected to be 7.68% in 2025 and 8.92% in 2026. However, subsequent projections show a slowdown: 3.05% in 2027 and a fall of -2.87% in 2028.

The valuation is also contained in metrics on revenues: the EV/Sales is 1.19x TTM and 1.31x FWD, when the sector is traded at 3.19x and 3.08x, respectively. The Price/Sales is even lower, at 0.63x TTM and 0.69x FWD, with a discount of more than 70% compared to the sector in both readings. This confirms that the market is punishing PRU not only for its low profit growth, but also for the perceived quality of its revenues.

On the growth side, the data is consistent with this caution. Revenue drops -0.13% YoY and is only expected to improve 4.09% FWD when the sector projects 6.39%. EBITDA dropped by 16.5% YoY and EBIT by 9.33%, although an EBIT recovery of 8.61% is expected in the next 12 months.

In terms of EPS, the adjusted data show a growth of 7.49% YoY and 8.40% FWD, although the structural growth projected for 3-5 years is just 7.66%, below the 9.54% sectoral.

Operating cash flow drops dramatically (-92.54% YoY), and although there is a rebound projection of 10.39% FWD, the reading is clear: cash generation is very weak. This is also reflected in a Price/Cash Flow TTM of 48.94x, well above the industry average (9.48x). The ROE figures grew 12.09% in the last year and are projected to have a 5.77% FWD, which is remarkable, but starts from a low base: the total ROE is only 7.95%, compared to 10.22% of the sector.

Overall profitability is weak. The gross margin is just 25.79%, while the sector stands at 60.63%. The EBIT margin is 4.54%, compared to 22.84% sectoral. The net margin accompanies this weakness, with just 3.80% against 22.50%. The return on assets (ROA) is minimal: 0.31% against 1.12% for the sector.

Even with a strong cash position ($38.06B) and an attractive dividend of 4.95%, leverage remains high, with a total debt of $47.71B and an EV of $71.91B. This financial structure contributes to the fact that the multiples EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT are extremely high: 25.49x and 26.25x TTM, although an improvement is projected with an EV/EBIT FWD of 8.11x.

Risks

Investing in PRU involves taking risks that go beyond numbers. As a financial and insurance company, your business is closely linked to the business cycle. In contexts of slowdown or volatility, such as the current one, PRU may face declines in revenues, pressure on margins, and increased regulatory demands.

The sensitivity to interest rates, inflation, and credit risk is structural in character, since a change in any of these variables can alter not only its profitability but also its balance sheet.

In addition, the sector is currently undergoing a profound transformation. Traditional models such as PRU increasingly compete with digital platforms, insurtechs, and lighter structures that operate at lower costs and with more flexibility. The scale that used to be an advantage can now become a brake if there is no real capacity for adaptation.

PRU also faces the challenge of managing long-term assets in uncertain markets. Its future commitments require a precise and conservative capital allocation, although it does not always have room to avoid exposure to market risk. Also, US regulations and globally are becoming increasingly demanding, which could affect their operations or raise compliance costs.

Finally, because these are very sensitive products — life insurance, pensions, long-term savings — any legal dispute, operational error, or reputational problem can have a very strong impact. It is for this reason that trust is a key asset for PRU, and to sustain it in a competitive and unstable environment is a risk in itself.

Conclusions

I consider PRU a solid and quite attractive investment today, trading with a P/E FWD below 8x and a P/B close to 1x. It is not an action for those who seek spectacular growth, but for those who value constant profitability, good capital management, and a clear dividend policy.

Therefore, I think it is an opportunity to buy with a medium to long-term approach. PRU doesn't have to reinvent itself; its true strength lies in consistency and prudence, which makes it a reliable bet in a market where stability is increasingly valuable.

data.title: T. Rowe Price: The S&P 500 V-Shape Recovery Is A Tailwind To Its Business (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
T. Rowe Price (NASDAQ:TROW) is an American asset management firm with $1.57 trillion in assets under management and is part of the exclusive list of dividend aristocrats, having increased its dividend for 39 consecutive years. This is my third time covering TROW, and in my latest rating in February, I considered it the right time to sell the shares due to their Q4 performance, during which the company displayed weakness despite favorable market conditions following Donald Trump's election.

Today, I see the company's outlook a bit brighter, and in this analysis, I will explain my reasons why the stock is no longer a "sell" and what situations have improved, allowing me to consider the stock a "hold."

T. Rowe Price Backdrop

in USD Q1 2025 Q1 2024 Q1 2023
Net flows -8.6B -8B -16.1B
Assets Under Management 1.57T 1.54T 1.34T

Source: Stock Analysis

First, although the assets under management of T. Rowe Price have increased since 2023 due to positive market performance, net flows have been negative during the past Q1s, indicating that investors are withdrawing more funds than they are depositing through T. Rowe Price's products. Ultimately, inflows are what drive the sustainability of the AUM, and this trend has been a sign of concern for T. Rowe Price investors. Nonetheless, the aspect that calms investors is that these cumulative outflows of $32 billion over the past three Q1s represent only a slight two percent of the company's AUM.

In million USD Q1 2025 Q1 2024 Q1 2023
Revenue 1,764 1,750 1,538
Revenue growth 0.8% 13.8%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Furthermore, negative outflows haven't represented negative revenue growth, as the effect of higher asset levels and AUM has led to a rise in management fees collected.

These negative outflows are being driven by secular trends in investors' preferences, which are transitioning away from open-ended mutual funds as a vehicle and active equity as an asset class. This has affected TROW, as a large portion of its AUM is in this format. As a result, management still expects negative outflows to continue in FY 2025. Yet, management has high expectations for flows within Fixed Income and Alternative Assets.

At the same time, to address the long-term headwinds, the company has begun launching products in an ETF format. For example, they have recently launched two transparent equity ETFs: Hedged Equity (THEQ) and Capital Appreciation Income (TCAL), bringing the family's total to 19 ETFs. The problem here is that the ETF business only has $12.5 billion in AUM, which is minor compared to the $773.4 billion in Equity AUM achieved in Q1, and therefore is unlikely to be a significant revenue contributor in the short term.

Stock Market Recovery & Financial Characteristics

The main reason for changing my previous "sell" rating to a "hold" is due to TROW's commission structure. Since February, with the stock market turmoil, the stock of T. Rowe Price suffered, as this is an equity-heavy asset manager, and lower asset levels inherently mean lower management fees for T. Rowe Price. Nonetheless, TROW has the advantage that most of its products are charged daily, which is a tailwind based on the V-shaped recovery of the S&P 500, with a modest drawdown duration of approximately four months. At the same time, in Q2, the market experienced a rapid rebound following the announcement of reciprocal tariffs, and since May, the index has been reaching quarterly highs.

Although T. Rowe Price is a consolidated asset manager with modest growth, they have appealing characteristics for income-oriented investors. First, this is a company with a strong balance sheet, totaling $2.837 billion in cash, representing 12.7% of its market capitalization, with no long-term debt. At the same time, they have a high dividend yield of 5.02%, which has had an appealing 10-year CAGR of 10.09%. Nonetheless, given the current outlook, it is unlikely that this dividend will rise at the same rate on a forward-looking basis, due to the challenges posed by active equity mutual funds. Simultaneously, the excess share repurchases over share issuance add a buyback yield of 0.73%.

Valuation
